description: Plants as the basis for ecological landscape design in urban and rural settings in late summer, fall, and early winter and their role in creating beautiful, resilient, and high performing outdoor spaces that enhance human health and well-being and provide a number of global ecosystem services.  Study plants in their native and designed habitats to understand the relationships between and among plants and their environment. Identify the aesthetic, structural, functional, and cultural characteristics of key Wisconsin native plants and a variety of non-invasive horticultural species as well as the composition, structure, and functions of  forest, wetland, and grassland communities. Express the essence and cultural meaning of plants and plant communities through hand and digital graphics and writing.

optimized_prerequisites.prerequisites_text: LAND ARC 250, (BOTANY 100,BOTANY/​BIOLOGY  130,ZOOLOGY/​BIOLOGY/​BOTANY  152, orBIOCORE 381and382), and sophomore standing, or graduate/professional standing
